Summary
The equipment delivered late pushed commissioning back three months.
The team running the factory test flagged the firmware issue.
Every figure supporting this recommendation comes from the site logs.
The units still in transit arrive next week.
João, thank you for making time. I've read that Line 4 has slipped, and my board wants one thing from this call: how bad, and can it be recovered.
Then let me start with the headline figure: a twelve-week slip, recoverable to four with the plan I'll walk you through. One late component caused it, and I need one decision from you today.
Good, that is what I needed first. Now the cause. What actually happened?

The findings point to one thing: the axle counters manufactured in Shenzhen arrived six weeks late, and two of the units delivered failed our inspection. I'll highlight the number that matters, six weeks from a single component.
But your engineers flagged a firmware mismatch in January. Why did that not stop the shipment?

That's a good question, let me think about that for a moment. The note raising the concern didn't reach the team handling the purchase order in time. I'd like to circle back to the exact date after the call, but the firmware fix is already approved.
Fair. So what do you actually need from us?

To wrap up: one slip, one cause, one plan. The call to action is a single decision, approve the night-shift access, and the integration testing postponed in March claws back eight weeks. I'll take any questions now, then send the note with the supporting data.
Let me start with the headline figure, then walk you through the findings and the recommendation.
The equipment delivered late was the single cause of the slip.
I'll draw your attention to one figure and let the handout carry the rest.
That's a good question, let me think about that for a moment, and I'll circle back to the detail.
To wrap up: one slip, one cause, one plan, and one decision I need from you today.

João, before you present to Nordic Rail this afternoon, let me give you the shape of it, because how you structure this matters as much as the numbers. You are walking a senior engineer through a three-month commissioning slip, and the instinct under pressure is to open with the apology and the detail. Do not. Open with the executive summary: one sentence on what happened, one on the cause, one on what you are asking for. The headline figure is the twelve-week slip, recoverable to four. Say that in the first minute, before anything else, because a busy client decides in the first minute whether to trust you. Then the findings, then the recommendation, in that order, every time. When you reach the supporting data, do not read the table out loud. Draw attention to the one number that matters and let the slide carry the rest. And leave the call to action clean: a single decision you need from them, not a list. If they interrupt with a hard question, that is good, not bad. Take it, answer the part you can, and if you need a moment, say so out loud. Silence you name is confidence; silence you hide is panic. You have the case. Present it like someone who already knows it is solid.

Three things before you present technical findings to a client who did not write the report. First, the audience is not you. You have lived inside this project for three months, so the cause is obvious to you; to them it is new. Walk them through it in the order they need, not the order it happened. State the finding, then the evidence, then what it means. The engineer who dumps the whole investigation on the table loses the room in ninety seconds. Second, the number does the work, not the sentence. When you highlight a key finding, put one figure on the slide and say it slowly. A recommendation supported by a single clear number is believed; a recommendation buried under twelve is not. Draw attention to the headline figure, and let everything else sit in the handout. Third, and this is the one engineers get wrong, manage the silence. When a hard question comes and you do not have the answer ready, do not fill the air with noise. Say, that is a good question, let me think about that for a moment. Then think. A pause you control reads as competence. Rushing to speak before you know what you are saying reads as fear. And if you genuinely need to check something, say you will circle back to it, and then actually circle back before the call ends. Presenting is not performing. It is making it easy for a busy person to say yes.
